response:
The error you're encountering is due to a mismatch between the version of the .NET runtime and the dependencies required by your WPF application. When running on Windows, your application is trying to load the PresentationFramework assembly version 4.0.0.0, which is part of .NET Framework, but your main application is built on .NET 7. This version mismatch results in the FileNotFoundException. To resolve this, you need to ensure that your WPF application is targeting a version of .NET that includes the necessary assemblies, such as .NET 7 or later, which includes the updated PresentationFramework.

One solution is to adjust the target framework of your WPF project to match the version of your main application. However, since your WPF project is not compiled when running on Mac, you need to ensure that it can still be dynamically linked. This means that your WPF project should be built with the same target framework as your main application, which is .NET 7. This will ensure that the required assemblies, such as PresentationFramework, are available at runtime on Windows. You can do this by updating the project file of your WPF project to target .NET 7 and rebuilding it.

Cousin to this, you need to make sure that your main application is correctly configured to load the WPF project dynamically. This involves setting up the necessary references and ensuring that the WPF project is built as a separate assembly that can be loaded at runtime. You might need to use reflection or some kind of dynamic loading mechanism to load the WPF assembly when the application is running on Windows. This approach allows your main application to remain cross-platform while still supporting WPF on Windows.

To further ensure compatibility, you should check the output path and build configuration of your WPF project. Make sure that the compiled WPF DLL is placed in a directory that is accessible by your main application when it runs on Windows. You can use a post-build event or a custom build step to copy the WPF DLL to the appropriate location. Additionally, you should verify that all dependencies of the WPF project are included in the output directory to avoid any missing files at runtime.

Finally, it's important to test your application thoroughly on both Windows and Mac to ensure that it behaves as expected. On Windows, confirm that the WPF window opens without any runtime errors, and on Mac, verify that the Cocoa-based UI is displayed correctly.
